operations.source_basis: 7-8-8: 'Terminate Class C service to aircraft landing at other than the primary airport at a sufficient distance from the airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate frequency for traffic and airport information.'

match.question_text: When does Class C service terminate for an aircraft landing at a secondary airport within the outer area?

replacements.question_text: When does Class C service terminate for an aircraft landing at a secondary airport within the outer area?
replacements.explanation: For an aircraft landing at a secondary airport, Class C service is terminated at a sufficient distance from the airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate frequency for traffic and airport information. The prescribed phraseology is CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED or CONTACT (facility identification).

choices.text: Class C service continues until the aircraft lands at the secondary airport.
Service is terminated at a sufficient distance from the secondary airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate advisory frequency.
Class C service never terminates for aircraft within the outer area, even after landing.
Service terminates automatically when the aircraft leaves Class C airspace proper, regardless of outer area position.

operations.problem: Explanation begins with 'Per 7-9-4b' and 'Per 7-9-4c' and ends with the interpretive gloss 'The weight threshold creates two distinct separation tiers,' which is not stated in the source blocks. The instruction and stem are clean; only the explanation needs correction.
operations.source_basis: 7-9-4b: VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft that weigh more than 19,000 pounds and turbojets by no less than 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual. 7-9-4c: VFR aircraft must be separated from all VFR/IFR aircraft which weigh 19,000 pounds or less by a minimum of target resolution except when ISR displayed, 500 feet vertical, or visual.

content.instruction: Identify the correct separation requirement for each scenario.
content.question_text: A Class B controller needs to separate VFR aircraft from two different IFR aircraft: an Airbus A320 (turbojet, over 19,000 lbs) and a Cessna 172 (under 19,000 lbs). Which separation methods are available for each?

choices.text: From the A320: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual. From the Cessna 172: target resolution (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual.
From both: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual; target resolution is available for both.
From the A320: 3 miles only. From the Cessna 172: 1.5 miles only.
From both: 500 feet vertical or visual only; radar miles separation is not available in Class B.

content.explanation: Per 7-9-4b, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing more than 19,000 lbs and turbojets by: 1.5 miles (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual. Per 7-9-4c, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing 19,000 lbs or less by: target resolution (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual. The weight threshold creates two distinct separation tiers.

content.instruction: Identify the correct separation requirement for each scenario.
content.question_text: A Class B controller needs to separate VFR aircraft from two different IFR aircraft: an Airbus A320 (turbojet, over 19,000 lbs) and a Cessna 172 (under 19,000 lbs). Which separation methods are available for each?

choices.text: From the A320: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual. From the Cessna 172: target resolution (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual.
From both: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual; target resolution is available for both.
From the A320: 3 miles only. From the Cessna 172: 1.5 miles only.
From both: 500 feet vertical or visual only; radar miles separation is not available in Class B.

content.explanation: In Class B airspace, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing more than 19,000 pounds and turbojets by no less than 1.5 miles (discontinue 1.5 NM separation when ISR is displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual separation. VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing 19,000 pounds or less by a minimum of target resolution except when ISR is displayed, 500 feet vertical, or visual separation.

operations.problem: Question stem contains parenthetical paragraph-number citations '(7-9-6)' and '(7-8-6),' teaching document-location trivia rather than testing operational knowledge of the separation exceptions. Explanation uses 'In Class B (7-9-6)' and 'In Class C (7-8-6a),' reinforcing the scaffolding.
operations.source_basis: 7-9-6: 'VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. Traffic advisories and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.' 7-8-6a: 'VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. Traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.'

match.question_text: How does the helicopter separation exception in Class B airspace (7-9-6) differ from the helicopter separation exception in Class C airspace (7-8-6)?

replacements.question_text: How does the helicopter and powered-lift separation exception in Class B airspace differ from the helicopter and powered-lift separation exception in Class C airspace?
replacements.explanation: In Class B airspace, VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. In Class C airspace, VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. The Class B exception is broader — it exempts separation from both VFR and IFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft, while the Class C exception exempts separation only from IFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft. Traffic information or advisories and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate in both airspace classes.

choices.text: There is no difference; both airspace classes contain identical helicopter separation exceptions.
Class B exempts VFR helicopters from separation from VFR or IFR helicopters/powered-lift; Class C exempts separation only from IFR helicopters/powered-lift.
Class C has no helicopter exception; only Class B provides helicopter separation relief.
Class C exempts all helicopter separation; Class B requires full separation for all helicopters.
